commentBody:
46A/47D was a Natick for an Aussie.  Both require a depth of US-knowledge that can only be built up by doing these crosswords, which is what I enjoy.
Two new facts learned today!

HA HA.
(April Fool 2?)
What a fun puzzle! Once I got the revealer, it was smooth sailing. So much fun, fresh fill, too. I personally enjoyed INKLING, which brought to mind Tolkien and Lewis’s literary group, the Inklings.

Lost a couple minutes for misspelling artisInal, and lImp didn’t stand out as a mistake. 

Great puzzle!

I love a Thursday puzzle with an innovative theme that makes you think (but not too hard!!!) — just like this one. (When I have to think too hard to get the theme right, a puzzle can start to feel like adding up a long column of numbers.)
Really easy for a Thursday yet fun. I don’t understand why that’s OK sometimes. Maybe it’s because I’m tired and want to go to bed. ‘Night! 😴

Our constructor notes:
   I created this puzzle thinking it could run on a Wednesday, so I was surprised that they accepted it for a Thursday.

Joe,
You and me both. Thanks for the fine Wednesday.
27D made me smile. When I was a small child and didn’t know all the rules, I liked going to the 0223 gas station.
w|t a fun Thursday puzzle! I |d fun solving it!

48A was a great clue but the theme was a bit underwhelming. It seems to me that, like a lot of recent puzzles, this one was riddled with entertainment trivia. I like trivia in a puzzle but let's not have too much from one category.
Should have allowed you to put the "HA" in literally as a rebus, in my opinion.  I spent far too long with the puzzle correctly sussed, but not acknowledged.

Yep. A very Wednesday-ish puzzle. The revealer wasn’t too tough, and once you had that in hand, the themers fell pretty quickly, along with the rest of the grid. Fun puzzle nonetheless!

Speaking of ships that have sailed, good luck to the Artemis II CREW on their JOY RIDE to the moon!
Thank you Sean and commenters.

Really liked the theme on this one, fun! WHALE SHARKS especially. I thought, oh cool, PODS is next to WHALE but then remembered a group of SHARKS is a shiver. ELS is close enough to EELS. And INKLING is fab fill that evokes OCTOPI.

I’ve solved today’s puzzle (without clues) - I’ve even compared to other solver’s answers - I’ve tried every combo of rebus/ no rebus and still the app shows mine is not solved - has anyone else experienced this?

Once again, on my android, the overlay or grid didn't work right for me. There were no dark bold lines. So for me, i was simply missing the HAs. Not very satisfying. Had to read the commentary to learn what it was supposed to look like. 
Fortunately it was an easy solve anyway.

Agree with Mr. Marquez that this felt Wednesday. Decent theme: among all lines (queue lines, gas lines, thin blue lines), laugh lines are the best. It's also nice that each of the themers can be said with an ironic ha ha chuckle: 

Heh heh heh ... what have we here? Heh heh [kinda creepy]

Whale ha ha ha sharks the size of schoolbuses??!!

Ha! THAT ship has sailed!

Ha. ha. ha. Thanks but no thanks!

Just want to say, I appreciate the variety and willingness of all the guest columnists on Wordplay to give it a shot. They've all been high quality spots, some terrific. I do feel like we are seeing that liminal time akin to when Trebek left Jeopardy and we had all those guest hosts, who gradually thinned down to a few contenders. Or will this continue into the distant future just like this?

(A question I've been asking a lot these days.)
Too complicated. Thursday puzzles have become more complicated and difficult.

Happy Passover to all who celebrate!
I enjoyed this. Yes, it could have been a Wednesday but at least I think the level of clueing today was a cut above what I’ve seen lately, and it took me long enough to get the theme and I had an a-HA moment when I did.
Had a lot of fond memories from some of these clues. I went to school in Boston within view of the iconic CITGO sign. I still say NEATO and am a consummate KAZOO player. My daughter in law is from Arizona and my ringtone for her is “Get your kicks on Route 66”. Mozart’s prolific output reminded me of the late Tom Lehrer’s classic line: “it’s a sobering thought that when Mozart was my age…he had been dead for three years”. 
Anyway I got a lot of laughs from this puzzle, thanks Joe!

Gosh. That was a good solve.  Seeing that I’m solving it on 4/1, I appreciate the joke. 

A solemn Holy Week and Blessed Passover to all. May we remember and honor the two greatest commandments this weekend and throughout the next year.
Nice one! Once I HAd the HAng of it, it fell into place.
Had MOUTHLINES for the whole puzzle bruhhh
I|d a great time with this puzzle. I just thought I'd s|re t|t.
Well, I solved it in a little over 24 minutes. Now I shall read Wordplay and see how I got the music (which surprised me).

Thank you NYT. 
Fun concept. brilliant execution of laugh with HA. 
I think our international friends will find this tough given the colloquial expressions. 
Just a little more bite with the clues and this would have been [chefs kiss].  

If I’m being picky.  
I feel like egg carton was a stretch for SAC. 
And WHALESHARKS wasn’t really congruous or necessary. Given there were three expressions and a revealer. 

But thank you again. Constructor. Editors. It was fun.
HA HA Good one!
A quickie.  Completed in half my average time.
Cute & fun, thanks to the constructor!
Nice! Great Thursday puzzle!
This was such a fun Thursday puzzle! Thank you!
So. Much. Fun! Thanks, Joe!
Dear lord, I love a Thursday!!

I enjoyed the trick. It took me quite a while to figure it out, too, so the solve felt like a true Thursday.

The fill gave me some trouble, and the crossing of the sport team and TV channel would have stumped me not so long ago, when I had not yet learned of UTES from these puzzles. I have no idea what the clue refers to, but once only the T was missing there, I guessed what the answer would be.

I don't get why a custodian's cloths are supposed to RAGS - could somebody please explain? The G at the crossing of RA_S and the mysterious CIT_O was the last square I filled.
Not much of a c/llenge, but it did make me laugh.

This felt like a worthy Thursday.
Nice!

And in our travels, I just noticed the lovely
comments on my looooong post yesterday
about my son’s voice on my Waze app.

Thanks for reading the whole thing guys. 
You rock.

Wow…

Well, as I expected at least one welcome message to our new columnist but didn’t read any, I will end my silence and take the lead to do so. Welcome, Sean. And good luck! The Onion is a piece of cake next to what you will get to taste here. 🧅 🍰

32D. ARTISANAL
Isn't that being rather judgemental?

...Myself knowing at least a few artists who are quite relaxed and tolerant.

Myself also always loving a good laugh (esp on April Fuels Day) and having learned the hard way that sometimes it's wiser to keep said laughter under wraps...

...and tickled to remember the Old Old days when the NYT ran a segment titled LAUGH LINES that one time earned me an Honorable Mention

Well played, Joe Marquez!!

I was surprised to see that the current reigning Jeopardy champion Jamie wagered over $44,000 last night and had the wrong answer! Of course, Jamie started with over $55,000 so he still won the game and remained champion.

The question was about Mark Twain. It asked about a quote of his. Something like: It's the one day of the year when we learn what we are the other 364 days.

The answer is April Fool's Day. Jamie guessed Labor Day.
Thanks! That was, for me, just what I hope to find on a Thursday!

Daunting at first, but the LAUGHLINES helped.

Got Naticked by the 46A/47D intersection. Never having heard of the Holy War, I immediately assumed it was the Army/Navy game, which I'll never watch even if T issues an executive order that I have to.

I had to guess on the food topped with lime and that soapy stuff; better in the puzzle than on my plate!

Thanks!
This Thursday puzzle was the epitome of what seems to be becoming the new normal. It was clever and fun, but ultimately not particularly challenging.  I enjoyed solving it, but I long for more.

HA HA HA 
TNK YOU
Welcome to Sean McGowan, a refreshing Wordplay columnist in the mode of Deb Amlen! I am looking forward to reading your lively columns in the days to come.

Missed opportunity for parallel cluing at 55A: [Fantasy warrior]=ORC. (And let's not get started on whether Star Wars is sci-fi, fantasy, or a whole 'nother genre.) On the other hand, it was fun to see Tolkien's name so close to INKLING. IYKYK. But only this morning did it occur to me that Tolkien, Lewis, et al.'s use of "inkling" could have been a play on words-->"ink"+the diminutive "ling," as in "duckling"-->"little inker"="scribbler." (The real word "inkling" comes from an archaic verb "to inkle"="to hint at." I inkle all the time, don;t you?) No laughing songs in Mozart, or in *Fidelio*, so we have to look a little later--here's VERY funny woman, glittering and being gay. (Too bad it's a still, as I'm sure the visual humor was hilarious!--and let's not get started on whether Candide is an opera, a musical, or a whole 'nother genre.) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FDtwp3cB2yU Despite getting the gimmick early on, the puzzle took me several minutes over my Thursday average, Mr. Marquez. YMMV

High props for originality here.  I don’t ever remember seeing this gimmick before (and please correct me, someone, if I’m wrong) – these thick black vertical lines, which act as rebuses.  

You know those rebuses that work one direction but not the other, and cause confusion simply because they only work one way?  Today’s thick black rebus lines can ONLY work across.

I bow down to Joe, coming up with something new in Crosslandia. Bravo, sir.

I also love the theme answers – all colorful, worthy, and all NYT answer debuts.  When they turned out to be so beautiful, it added to my appreciation.

Speaking of beauty: INKLING!  ATISINAL!  JOYRIDE!  KAZOO!

One more thing.  I often report on my attempts to guess the revealer after filling in the theme answers, which are mostly failures.  But today, my friends, I popped it right in without a single cross.  If you tell me that task was easy today, I will not hear you, as I am cocooned in my joy.

So tHAt HAppened.  A lovely cap to a lovely outing.  Thank you Joe!

The first theme answer came very quickly for me, and the second even faster. And that's when I realized that all the lines were going to be HA. Then I had LINES at the end of the revealer, so the answer had to be LAUGH LINES. While I understand that this was a fairly U.S.-centric puzzle, there were enough little ol' crosswordese fills to allow non-US solvers to complete it. For me, it was okay, but way too simple for a Thursday.

A lovely theme answer, combining the double-rebus HA, and its suggestion of laughter, would be:
-
-
-
-
-
-
-
-
-
C/rlie C/plin
Good thursday fun.

A fun gimmick with not so fun fill. Some answers were a bit of a stretch — hopefully they won’t result in any more lines on our faces. I’m probably never going to fully love a crossword puzzle that doesn’t use full words as answers. The gibberish just ain’t fun for me, and feels cheap. The NE corner was impenetrable for me. Overall not bad, but not a favorite. 

Happy Thursday.

This should have been yesterday’s puzzle. Easy enough for a Wednesday, a much better theme, and the added fun “laughs” for April Fools’ Day. The biggest challenge today was figuring out if (and how) we have rebus squares, and ultimately catching on (from the revealer) that we don’t.

Fun Thursday puzzle. Typical slow start for me, but finally tumbling to the trick was a huge turning point and that's always a nice touch. And all four of the theme answers are fairly familiar terms or phrases but all of them are making their debut in puzzles. Just a bit surprised by that. 

And... a puzzle find today. I'll put that in a reply. 

...
Can't wait for next year -- April 1 will be a Thursday.

Bravo! An excellent Thursday puzzle, which truly puzzled me (though, as always, I intentionally avoided looking at the revealer until near the end of the puzzle). Solved it without assistance, but took about half an hour, with more than a bit of reworking as I went. In retrospect, I should have seen the theme much earlier than I did, since I got W|T|VEWEHERE quickly and even considered what letters were missing. Just didn't click when it should have.

Liked "Player on the bench?" for PIANIST, which I don't think I've seen before (though I imagine it has appeared previously).

All in all, a winner of a Thursday puzzle.

Earworm for the day  ...

IF I |D A |MMER

Oh, and one more somewhat appropriate puzzle find today. A Friday from April 4, 1975 by Hugh McElroy. Two 15 letter theme answers in that one: OMAHAHAHAHAHAHA IDAHOHOHOHOHOHO Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=4/4/1975&g=20&d=A ....

Welcome to Sean McGowan! I look forward to reading more from him. (Brief moment of confusion as I thought he might be that guy from the Pogues, then remembered that that guy from the Pogues had died a couple years back.)

Favorite answer today: W|LES|RKS. They're my second-favorite shark, behind the Greenland shark.

Of course I was wedging the HAs into the squares until I got to the Reveal and realized I could just have pretended the LAUGHs were in there.  But that's not how I roll; gimme a challenge, and I'll Make It Work.  We quilters learn all the "fudge it" tricks of the trade.  
Today I Learned....that Batman wore a COWL.  Oh, really?  I had CAPE, of course, which worked fine with the CULTS, but not so well for the 13A entry, which forced me to rethink...  

In the end, this was rather easier than the Tricky Thursdays that have made me sweat, but I was grateful, being in a Weakened Condition--up since 4:30 because of the ITCHing from abut a dozen stings:  punishment for disturbing some Mississippi Native Fire Ants (not nearly as lethal as the Red Imported Fire Ants who swarm en masse to attack.)  The MNFA don't make hills, so they're harder to spot/avoid.  Weeding is hazardous to your health.  

Oh, the puzzle:  well, I thought it was fun.  I'm glad to say my LAUGH LINES are deeper than the Worry Wrinkles--although I don't  know why.  
Seventeen Days 'til Seattle!  Woo hoo!
As an avowed hater of Thursday puzzles... I loved this!!! Had a lot of fun with it.

If you click onSean McGowan’s byline, this is his description:

“I’m a writer for The New York Times’s Games team. I contribute to the Wordplay column and the Gameplay newsletter.
What I Cover
I write about games in all forms, though I specialize in crosswords and other language puzzles. You can find me demystifying The Times’s late-week crosswords in the daily Wordplay column or contributing to the weekly Gameplay newsletter.
My Background
I’m a comedy and games writer who joined The Times in 2026. I’ve contributed headlines and jokes to satirical news outlets like The Onion and ClickHole, and penned humor pieces for The New Yorker’s Shouts and Murmurs.

In addition to covering games, I’ve written a few myself. I’ve served on editorial teams for video game developers, board game companies and online chess apps. I also construct my own crosswords and variety puzzles.

For me, there’s nothing more satisfying than popping open the hood of a puzzle, joke or game to discover its mechanics. I suspect that curiosity is what led me to earn a B.S. in electrical engineering from Penn State, though I’ve since swapped wires for words. They’re generally safer.”

Hi Sean! I was away for a bit and missed my opportunity to bid fondest farewells to Deb (alas), but at least I can be here to welcome you. I love the Onion and can't wait for your contributions. 

In re: the constructor's comment, I agree with the editorial decision to run this fun & chewy puzz on a Thursday. Gimmicky Wednesdays get the commentariat all atwitter, but more to the point, IM(H)O, this fill felt challenging enough to warrant late-week publication. 

For some reason, I stayed stubbornly in the NW until I'd figured out the bolded lines, leaving the revealer as a punchline rather than a clue. This made for a devilish mini-puzz (COWL...), but then zipping through the other theme entries once I'd figured it out was hugely satisfying.

Also, what a clever way to fit those longer spanners into a 15x15 grid. Thanks, Joe Marquez, Sean, & eds.!

Pretty good Thursday puzzle today.  Though I prefer when puzzles have letters removed they spell something else without the letters.  Once I figured out it was LAUGH LINES and not FAULT LINES the puzzle fell into place.  Generally very good cluing, all indirect figure-outables with less proper nouns and niche trivia.

My only "Something's amiss" at the end is that I had MINI instead of TINY, not realizing it was PORGY and not PORGI.

Today I completed the crossword putting in all the rebuses. It told me i was close but had one or more errors. I checked it against the solution but couldn't find a mistake. Not until I took out all the rebuses leaving just the first letter did it say it wa s correct. I don't recall other rebus puzzles acting that way. Can't the rebuses stay in usually and you still have a correct solution?
Great Thursday. Fun gimmick with real phrases as answers. For some reason I got a real kick out of INKLING crossing with AGLET. Twelve stars.

Decent puzzle; the theme was fairly easy to figure out.

My personal pet peeve though is when there's two crossing American-focused clues that can't really be deduced, like UTES and TBS. The T was the last square I filled out which I only did by trying every letter on the keyboard

First, welcome to Crosslandia, Sean McGowan.  The two things I look for in a good Wordplay columnist is a love of crosswords and a sense of humor.  It looks like you have both.  I wish you every success in your new position.

Second, thanks to Joe Marquez and the puzzle editors for a nice Thursday-worthy puzzle.  Thursdays have conventionally(?) been my favorite day to do NYTimes crosswords.  I thrill at the excitement of what new trick the constructors will have for us.  
Lately I've worried that perhaps those days were gone and that there were no new tricks to be found.  Were the constructors running out of ideas, or had I just been solving for too long?
But today my faith in Thursday is being restored.  Good theme, good level of difficulty, good revealer, and, best of all, good gimmick! 
HA HA!  I'm not laughing at you, I'm laughing with you!
T|NKS again, Joe Marquez!

My first thought when I read the revealer was ‘elevens’ or the vertical lines between the eyebrows. Prior to my devotion to a competent injector, I called them my ‘Mad Mommy Wrinkles’. 

Fun solve! Welcome to Sean!

Wise / Crack – ouch. That hurt!

Thanks for the fun one Mr. Marquez!

Was I having fun yet?  Quite the opposite.  So I skipped down to the revealer clue, which was no help at all.  The only answer I could come up with was CREASES and that solved exactly nothing.  I would have to soldier on and get the actual answer, wouldn't I?

It was sort of clear what each theme answer was meant to be -- but it was not at all clear how to get there.  At least not to me.

But when LAUGH LINES finally came in, it was a relief.  At first I tried to insert the word LAUGH.  Silly girl!  But as soon as I thought of HAHA, the scales dropped from my eyes.  It all worked!!  It all made sense!!

For me, this was a puzzle that was an annoying and perplexing slog before I understood the gimmick and became quite enjoyable once I did.  The gibberish-y nature of the grid fill-ins was sort of unpleasant, actually.  The Aha Moment was really nice, though.  I never know exactly HOW I feel about puzzles like this.  They're a mixed bag for me.

Thank you, Joe, for what felt like a nice Thursday solve to me despite your thoughts that it should have fallen on a Wednesday. Your clever HAHA clueing has even added to my LAUGH LINES.😂

Welcome, Sean, as our new XW columnist! Given your comedic background, I look forward to your future columns infused with humor that is so often appreciated (and needed) at the end of the week!😊

About 30 years ago, my husband and I walked into a record store (remember those?) in search of a CD of PORGY and Bess as a gift for my mother-in-law. The young employee asked if that was a release from a new indie band.

This was a joy to solve.  Unique theme and pleasant theme answers that I don't recall seeing in a puzzle before.  Especially. 36A, truly a charismatic megafauna; count yourself as lucky if you've seen one in the wild.

NOT FAIR.  I used rebuses and they were all correct, but rebuses were, for some reason, not allowed.  I call foul. The puzzle should have been solvable either way, but no, it was not. This broke my 60 day streak with no look-ups.  Oh, I know, I should have tried removoing the rebuses. I know, I know.  But now, I'm just a little smarter, so hopefully it won't happen again.  Still, I don't like it. Grrrr
I liked the theme but some of the other clues were 🙄. Looks like a backward 3? Really? Are you a child?

It finally happened! 

Got the trick while solving the puzzle and used that new found knowledge to complete the solve. 

I may quit Thursday puzzles while I am ahead.
I enjoyed the theme and the Thursday trick, but a lot of the clues were lackluster and too heavy on trivia from my taste.
HA HA HA! That was a JOYRIDE. Fine Thursday gimmick, not too easy, although I was thinking HAT was the key to it.

Such fun. Took a while to get the gimmick but slowly worked out we were missing HA. Had smileLINES before LAUGH. 

Happy Easter to those who celebrate. We stick to the Pagan rituals of blessing the fruit trees (similar to the Wassail in January), then stuffing our faces with huge chocolate eggs. Yum.

Cool theme! 

I don’t accept “what’ve we here” as a valid variation of that phrase. Confident I’ve never heard it phrased with a contraction like that.  I also found it odd to say 
“thanks but no thanks” is sarcastic, I never thought of it that way, maybe a little pithy but genuine!
Fun solve. Kudos to the author.
Great job Joe! This must have taken a lot of noodling but was well worth it for us solvers.
Fine, I guess. Glad others seem to have enjoyed it. Welcome, Sean!

"Knock, knock. It’s your newest Wordplay columnist, Sean McGowan."

If indeed Sean McGowan is the (or a?) new Wordplay columnist rather than the latest guest columnist, I would have expected somebody higher up on (what used to be called) the masthead to introduce him. But I'm an old fog(e)y who started newspapering with typewriters and hot metal.
Terrible puzzle...s(ha)me on you NTY - was able to solve with the down clues but seriously flawed puzzling here.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
This puzzle made me chortle when all was filled and done.

Some cluing, like in the northeast corner, initially seemed disappointingly basic but turned out to be anything but given the surfeit of possibilities before any toeholds.  For 12D, for example I started with DROVE, switched to IRATE (which worked with OMAR) and only settled on IRKED when it became clear that 18A's "Jerk" was not looking for an insult.  Similar instances throughout.

And while it's true that I would give her a kidney if she were to need and ask for one - and maybe even if she just wanted one for kicks, so slavishly am I devoted to her mind - and that I look for her influence everywhere,  I defy anyone to tell me that 9D and 31A were not well and truly Weintraubianly clued.

On the other hand, the recent spate of late-week gimmes gave us 28A "Bungee jumping requirement" = CORD (that was a stretch!) and 14A "Team behind the cameras" = CREW (Monday wants its ball back), so there's some simple clueing which actually was just that.

And my next and oft-trotted-out quibble, shared by many among you, is the word-salad products of the gimmick.  Real live vocables between the end zones always make for wor(l)ds more elegant fields of play.

In the end, I think, altogether more of a JOYRIDE Thursday grid than many have been of late.

So, thanks setitors and pardon my portmanteau.

It's been some little bit of time since we've had a rebus in the original sense of the rebus puzzles I solved when a child in the 60s, with a picture to be interpreted as a word or vice versa. I've gotten so used to associating "rebus" with "hit the ESC key" that it took me a moment to realize what Sean was talking about.
Pretty poor cluing... Still miffed on several clues that I gave up on. Even after revealing I'm still confused. Rough solve despite a cool theme.

Nothing more disappointing than filling in your puzzle and getting the "Not quite!" pop up. Couldn't make the connection on the theme and I gave up at that point and checked the puzzle answers, then I didn't understand the theme until I read the column. Bummer! I think if I'd let it sit for a while I could have cracked it...gotta learn some patience.
I've been known to complain about a Thursday puzzle now and then, but no complaints today. I liked it.

This was a clunky one. Mostly, it needed a visual design tweak: the rebus squares are very poorly marked. 

1) Because there were two in each clue, using a heavy border on just one side of the square makes it look like brackets around several squares, not individual squares.  

2) Even when I knew it was single squares, I couldn’t always tell which square was highlighted. For example, it looks like the N in SCAN is a rebus square. It’s not, but the rebus indicator borders both squares equally. 

3) It didn’t help that these were the kinds of rebuses that only worked one way, causing gibberish the other way. I dislike these. 

So how do we solve this design problem?

A simple gray background would have been much better but I understand why we want a line here - laugh lines! 

So then maybe a series of light gray lines, for rebus squares. You get to keep the “laugh lines” theme; in fact it makes MORE sense because now you have laugh lineS, plural. 

Just my $0.02.

What do we hope to gain by writing some words about the daily crossword? 
For me, it's sometimes an impulse to provide feedback on aspects of puzzles that I find objectionable, in the no doubt vain hope that someone at NYT will listen; or that at least someone else will say "yes, I share that objection." 
Whenever I have posted such a critique, I face now-predictable responses: "if you don't like it, you're incompetent." "You are too gloomy." "Only positive comments are welcome." "You don't *get* it." "You are in the minority and therefore not worth hearing from." 
Disheartening. Like being in a family, saying that you don't like the meatloaf, and being told that don't have the wit to understand meatloaf, so go live somewhere else. (The dish, not the musical artist)
Today, I am hoping for better. Any respondents: please consider that disagreeing with my position does not mean I deserve a lecture in the history of crosswords or the nature of Thursdays. 
My objection is, once again, that the NYT has moved in the past decade to gimmicks that cost too much in terms of puzzle integrity. 
A completed puzzle with "WTVEWEHERE" "WLESERKS" looks utterly absurd, as though the emperor is wearing no clothing and we're not allowed to say so -- because this is all justified by the "laugh lines" key. 
It just isn't, for me. There are lines, I'm supposed to substitute "ha"when I see one. Got it. 
It's still absurd. The integrity of the crossword is being sacrificed to an unfunny gimmick.
As to your "facial wrinkles"...  When I told my wife they were laugh lines, she said "Nothing's that funny!" HAHA
Only complaint was the 9 across clue “sci-fi warrior”…I got it in the end, but Star Wars is fantasy, not science fiction. :)
Very clever Thursday puzzle. Thank you, Mr. Marquez!
That was a lot of fun! Thanks!
Good fun and clever theme. Discovering  it was  truly an a-HA moment.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ a lost day … too late now
for the egg carton show
too late
to do anything about it
one has to do anything and so
d/let this day go in a whisper with
a/what tiny thanks we have

Q. W|TWAST|T?

A. A fun puzzle.

What a fun puzzle! I really enjoyed it!! 

Probably for a first time ever, I got the revealer without any crossings, or at least for the first time ever I was right....  I'm pretty sure there have been a time or two when I put something without crosses in, but was wrong. 

I did initially think of using the rebus box, but I've learned to be patient and just wait for the puzzle to reveal to me how I should enter. It's like using the force, Luke, but completely different in every single way. 

Well, save for one way... I mean, I am kind of a hero. If you don't believe me, just ask me.

Anyhow, quick for me, but very fun! Kind of wish it could have been a big Sunday puzzle so I could have gotten more LAUGHLINES.

And speaking of LAUGHLINES, welcome, Sean!!
Whale Sharks for the win!
I think Yoda would object to the characterization of Jedis as warriors (9A)
The Jedi are fantasy warriors, not sci-fi. The Force is literally magic.

Did anyone else have MINI for 64A, and spend 10 minutes looking for errors while glossing over the apparently believable incorrect crosses of PORG(I) and L(M)E? 

...no? just me? cool cool cool

I appreciate the effort and construction,  but these complicated fills make it near impossible when I'm looking for typos.

Meh!
Took me a bit, but I got there.  Slowly learning and getting better.  I feel I’ll get a clean Thursday solve sometime soon.  Enjoyable theme and revealer.
So by putting the HA in the rebus means I don't get a win?

Really should have accepted the rebus in the fill. The final fill looks ugly either way though. I'm not an anti-rebus/gimmick solver but I do think we've lost something when nonsense words end up in the final grid

The theme was fun to suss out, but a lot of the fill seemed easy.

I get that the NYT varies difficulty to appeal to a variety of people, but I would have liked a bit more of a challenge.

I enjoyed this puzzle. The clues were pretty straightforward -- [Followers of articles] made me smile -- and there were enough for both the older crowd (like me) and younger solvers. I agree with Joe Marquez that it could have been published on either a Wednesday or a Thursday. To me, it felt somewhere in between.

That said, I am truly surprised by the number of people who seem not just annoyed but offended by the laugh lines gimmick.  Holy laugh lines, Batman! This is supposed to be a puzzle, not a paint-by-numbers canvas or a fill-in-the-blanks quiz. Part of the fun is trying to figure out what devious strategies the creator has used to make it a challenge, especially toward the end of the week. If today's puzzle didn't work for you, well .... there's always tomorrow.

This was a fun puzzle. But I had rebuses in the grids that failed the completion check. I ended up clicking on check puzzle and realized that I had to clear the extra letters from the cells. That caused me to lose the streak :(

I tried botox once a couple years ago. Not for laugh lines, but for my forehead wrinkles. Before, I noticed how nice and smooth people’s foreheads looked. It made me very excited to embrace my hard-earned laugh lines and expression for years to come.

@Andrzej  The two college rival teams are the University of Utah and Brigham Young University, both in Utah, not far from each other. Utah is named for the Native American Ute nation, hence UTE. BYU is a private university, steeped in the Mormon tradition, while U of Utah is state-run and is secular, but I think has a rather high number of Mormon students. Hence the “holy war” aspect.

The Polish word "dozorca" appears to be the best equivalent of the English custodian understood as a "guardian" of a property. Before 1989, all housing estates had a "dozorca" who would take general care of the property, clean the corridors, mow the lawns, do minor repairs, etc. These days building administrators outsource the various roles onto specialized contractors, and the traditional "dozorca" is no more.

However, the Polish "dozorca" may not refer to a custodian in the wider sense. I would never call the custodian of a certain legacy a "dozorca" - they would be a "piastun", probably. 

It seems my language has a much more specilized and granular terminology when it describes people responsible for certain things.

The English cleaning rag would be our "szmata", a word with an interesting, ancient Slavic etymology, referring, among others, to things that have been torn to pieces. Generally one expects a "szmata" to be a repurposed piece of cloth. Figuratively speaking, "szmata" rudely  refers to a person who has betrayed some ideals, and "zeszmacić się" ("to become a rag") means this process of betrayal of ideals. For example, during our latest illiberal turn in politics of 2015-2023, many lawyers "zeszmacili się", supporting the government's assault on democratic, Constitutional ideals.

An item specifically made to wipe stuff - rather than repurposed for the role - is a "ścierka" ("ścierać" means "to wipe").

@MB

Thanks! That brings important context to the clue. I love it when members of this community take the time to explain stuff to others. I appreciate it 🙂

@Andrzej 

Before paper towels existed, a common use for worn-out clothing, towels, etc. was as cleaning rags. The term "rag" has been passed on to any cloth used for cleaning purposes, even if that cloth is clean, new, and specifically manufactured for that purpose.

@Steve L

*minor spoilers ahead*

Sorry you ran into a streak buster today! But ultimately, @Andrzej is right -- if you fill in the squares with all the letters using a rebus, it completes the phrases for across but messes up the downs.

E.g., if you put "wha" into the beginning of 16A, it changes your answer for 1D to "coWHAl", which is clearly not a part of Batman's costume. Alternately, if you put "hatha" into the second square, it changes your answer for 2D to "upHATHAo", which doesn't work either.

@Nora it’s essentially a simplified cursive E. And ESSO is Standard, literally, as the brand name for Standard Oil of New Jersey, later Exxon in the US, although the ESSO brand was retained in Canada (fortunately for the Times crossword). The original Standard Oil, founded by John D. Rockefeller, was one of the first monopolies broken up under the Sherman Anti-Trust Act in 1911, back when the US government actually did such things.

Seems like you're completely missing the point. there are no rebus squares. The rebus element is that each of the heavy borders (each LAUGHLINE) represents the letters HA. Because the HA is in the line and not in the square, it does not appear in the down answers, meaning they are not gibberish at all.

@Bob T. That makes a lot of sense and addresses my rebus complaint. There weren’t any. 

I officially rescind my earlier criticism; I still think it was a bit clunky but overall, a solid Thursday puzzle.
Right, Bob T. Only in Crosslandia does "rebus" (also) mean squishing two or more letters into one square.
@Bob T. Knowledge!

@Asher B.

As you are moved to comment on puzzles you don't like, I am moved to comment on commenters I disagree with.
What I disagree with most in your comment is your desire to pre-empt criticisms of your criticism.
What I disagree with next is the tension, if not contradiction, between your desire to have an influence on future puzzles with your (apparent) disregard for the opinions of your fellow puzzle solvers.

I criticize puzzles often; indeed, I criticized yesterday's puzzle, and many others here are famous for their difficulty in being pleased by the day's puzzle.  
But I don't tell anyone how they should respond to my criticism.

In conclusion, I disagree with your conclusions.  I found the gimmick fun and original.  You have every right to say that this puzzle is not for you.  It is absurd, however, to go on to say that the "integrity" of the puzzle is being "sacrificed", especially in light of the number of comments which disagree with your conclusion.

I'm sorry if this interferes with your hope for a better reception.

@Asher B. - I have literally never, not once, in many years here, seen most of the comments you claim to have seen posted here (for example, "only positive comments are welcome").

If you truly have a valid point, how about posting truthfully, rather than making up straw-man arguments about comments that have never been made? 

On the other hand, I have seen many, many ridiculous comments saying things like "nobody ever says that" (about a crossword entry that I and other have used and heard many times) or "that is not a word" (about a term that is in all dictionaries) or "Do better" (from someone who should take their own advice). 

You seem to believe that YOU are allowed to voice your criticisms, however ridiculous and invalid, but nobody is allowed to rebut your criticisms. Sorry, that is not how life works.

FYI, many of us have also criticized the puzzles recently -- most because they're being dumbed down to the point where they are often completely unchallenging to all but the most novice of solvers. THAT is something worth complaining about.

The rebuses in this puzzle -- [LAUGH] LINES representing "HA" -- are what everyone else in the universe calls a rebus. Only in Crosslandia does "rebus" also mean squishing two or more letters into one square. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/rebus
